arguments_against: The strongest case against: the biography carries effectively a single initial in staff-list terms ('A. Abramson'), and the 1921 staff list prints 'A. A. Abramson' with a double initial, suggesting a possibly different man with a middle name; meanwhile the deterministic pass failed every colony/tenure/position/edition check, and the compiled timeline places the biography's later posts in Transjordan, not Palestine. This fails on inspection. The colony FAIL is a parsing artifact: the timeline mechanically inherited 'Transjordan' from the single 1921 'ch. Br. rep., Transjordan' clause, but the biography's surrounding career (mily. gov., Hebron, 1918; land commission and land department from 1920) is the Palestine administration, and Hebron sits in Palestine's southern district. The namesake hypothesis requires a second A. Abramson in Palestine whose career independently tracks the biography through FOUR distinct, specific, senior posts in the same order at the right dates: chairman of the land commission (bio 1920 / staff list 1921), district governor of the southern district (bio 1922 / staff list 1923-24), district commissioner of the northern district (bio 1925 / staff list 1927-28), and commissioner of lands (bio 1927 / staff list 1929-32). The 1-2 year staff-list lag is the normal gap between appointment date and edition compilation. Corpus context shows exactly one official and one biography with this surname, so no live same-name candidate exists, and these are senior named offices, not generic junior titles. The 'A. A.' in 1921 is a one-off against eight subsequent years of 'A. Abramson' and is most plausibly a typesetting/OCR duplication.

evidence_cited.fact: ohmn., land coman., and dir., land dept., 1920

would_overturn: Discovery of a second Abramson (e.g. a different forename behind 'A. A. Abramson') serving in the Palestine land administration in the 1920s; or evidence that Albert Abramson's 1920-1927 posts were held in Transjordan's separate establishment while a different Abramson held the identically-sequenced Palestine posts; or a death/retirement record for Albert Abramson before 1932 that contradicts the stint's later years.
notes: Deterministic checks all FAILed on place because the timeline compiler inherited 'Transjordan' from the 1921 'ch. Br. rep., Transjordan' clause into events 4-6; reading the verbatim entry shows the career is the Palestine administration (Hebron 1918, land commission/land dept 1920, southern and northern district posts, commissioner of lands). The edition_cooccurrence FAIL is likewise colony-gated; the bio (1927-1940) and stint (1921-1932) in fact co-occur in editions 1927-1932. Single-initial warning satisfied by a strong independent dimension: four specific senior position matches in identical sequence with consistent 1-2 year staff-list lag, in a corpus with only one Abramson official and one Abramson biography.

stint_ids: Adams, P. F. B___Orange River Colony___1905
arguments_against: Strongest case against: 'Burnet Adams' on the Legislative Council could be a different Adams (a relative, or another of the 96 Adams officials), and Frederick Bradford Adams also passes the initials gate for both ORC stints ('B' for Bradford). This fails because the Legislative Council record names Burnet Adams explicitly as 'Surveyor-General' in 1905-1907, the very years P. F. B. Adams is listed as Surveyor-General in the Survey and Mines Department of the same colony; a colony has one Surveyor-General, so these are two listings (departmental plus ex-officio council seat) of the same office-holder, and 'Burnet' matches the bio's forename 'Burnett'. The P. F. B. stint itself matches the bio on exact initials, the unique senior office ('survr.-gen., O.R.C., July, 1902'), and the mines duty ('in addition head of mines dept., Apr., 1903' vs 'Acting Chief Inspector of Mines'). Frederick Bradford Adams was 22 in 1905, was in education before his 1908 S. Nigeria appointment, and his bio shows no South African service at all; he is not a live claimant for a Surveyor-Generalship.

evidence_cited.stint_id: Adams, P. F. B___Orange River Colony___1905
evidence_cited.fact: Surveyor-General and Acting Chief Inspector of Mines

evidence_cited.stint_id: Adams, P. F. B___Orange River Colony___1905
Adams, P. F. B___Orange River Colony___1905
evidence_cited.fact: in addition head of mines dept., Apr., 1903

would_overturn: Evidence that the Orange River Colony had a separately appointed Surveyor-General named Burnet Adams distinct from P. F. B. Adams in 1905-1907 (e.g., a Blue Book or gazette showing two different men holding survey offices), or a record showing P. F. B. Adams was absent/replaced in those years.
notes: The two Orange River Colony stints assigned to adams_philip-francis-burnett_e1902 are almost certainly double-listings of one office-holder: the departmental entry (Survey and Mines Department) and an ex-officio Legislative Council seat, both titled Surveyor-General in the same colony and years. The reviewer may wish to merge them at the stint level. The NSW Surveyor-General P. F. Adams is plausibly the historically known Philip Francis Adams of New South Wales, a distinct earlier individual with no biography in this case.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: the dossier notes this stint is also gate-compatible with an outside biography, 'adshead_percy-williams_b1892'. 'Percy Willetts' and 'Percy Williams' are one OCR slip apart, so either (a) they are duplicate biographies of the same man, in which case this assignment is harmless, or (b) there were two distinct P. W. Adsheads, and the Uganda Chief Accountant could be the other one. Scenario (b) would make this a false merge. Why it likely fails: the biography in this case explicitly prints 'ch. acctnt., P.W.D., Uga., 1926' — the exact position (Chief Accountant), exact department (Public Works Department), and exact colony (Uganda) of the stint, with an appointment date two years before the first staff-list appearance in 1928. The bio also places him on the 'business profits comttee., Uga., 1928', the stint's first listed year, and his departure to Palestine in 1937 is consistent with the staff listings ending by 1933. Two distinct P. W. Adsheads both holding the singular post of Chief Accountant, P.W.D., Uganda in the same years is not a realistic hypothesis. The residual risk is confined to the Willetts/Williams ambiguity, which is why this is 'probable' rather than 'certain'. The position_sim FAIL is a mechanical artifact: the scorer compared 'mem. of business profits comttee.' against 'Chief Accountant' instead of the bio's actual P.W.D. accountant event.

evidence_cited.fact: mem. of business profits comttee., Uga., 1928

would_overturn: Evidence that 'adshead_percy-williams_b1892' is a distinct person (not an OCR variant of Percy Willetts) whose own printed career claims the Chief Accountant, P.W.D., Uganda post in 1926-1933 — e.g. a verbatim entry for Percy Williams Adshead naming that office, or records showing two P. W. Adsheads serving in Uganda simultaneously.
notes: Single-bio, single-stint case. Strong multi-dimensional match: exact position + department + colony ('ch. acctnt., P.W.D., Uga., 1926' vs staff-list 'Chief Accountant', 'Public Works', Uganda), initials P.W. agree, and tenure 1928-1933 fits between the 1926 appointment and the 1937 Palestine transfer. Held at 'probable' rather than 'certain' solely because of the flagged outside-case gate-compatible biography 'adshead_percy-williams_b1892' (likely an OCR duplicate, Willetts/Williams); recommend the reviewer check whether that bio is a duplicate of this one and reconcile the two entity keys.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: with 96 Adams officials in the staff lists, another F. B. Adams could have served as a district officer in Nigeria, or the stint could be P. F. B. Adams with the leading initial dropped (his initials also pass the gate). This fails because P. F. B. Adams's entire printed career is South African survey work, ending as 'survr.-gen., O.F.S., 31st May, 1910', with no Nigeria service and a wholly different profession; and the stint matches Frederick Bradford on every specific dimension: initials F. B. exact, colony (his career is entirely Southern Nigeria), exact rank ('Second Class District Officer' in 1918 vs the bio's '2nd cls. dist. offr.' from 1914), province (Southern Provinces vs S. Nigeria), and the stint span 1915-1919 sits inside his attested 1914-1924 tenure in exactly that grade. The deterministic position_sim FAIL is an abbreviation artifact, not a real disagreement. The 'Thirty Second Class District Officers' entries are OCR garbling of the same rank heading.
would_overturn: A staff list or biography showing a second F. B. Adams serving concurrently as a district officer in the Nigerian provinces 1915-1919, or evidence that Frederick Bradford Adams was seconded away from Nigeria during those years.
notes: The two Orange River Colony stints assigned to adams_philip-francis-burnett_e1902 are almost certainly double-listings of one office-holder: the departmental entry (Survey and Mines Department) and an ex-officio Legislative Council seat, both titled Surveyor-General in the same colony and years. The reviewer may wish to merge them at the stint level. The NSW Surveyor-General P. F. Adams is plausibly the historically known Philip Francis Adams of New South Wales, a distinct earlier individual with no biography in this case.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: this is a single-initial biography ('ADAMSON, E') with 22 same-surname officials in the staff lists, so the namesake trap applies, and two other biographies (adamson_alfred-edward_b1871, adamson_st-john-ernest_b1867) are gate-compatible with this stint. The deterministic pass also failed on colony, tenure overlap, and position similarity, suggesting no placed agreement. Why this fails: the deterministic FAILs are an artifact of the timeline compiler mis-inheriting 'Malaya' — the verbatim printed entry explicitly moves the career to Singapore ('senr. exec. engrn., P.W.D., S'pore, 1955') and then states 'D.D.P.W., 1958-60', which expands exactly to the stint's 'Deputy Director of Public Works' in Singapore, listed 1959-1960. Deputy Director of Public Works is a unique senior post in a small colony, not a generic junior title, so a second E. Adamson holding it in the same years is not a realistic counter-hypothesis. The two alternative biographies were born in 1871 and 1867, making them 88 and 92 in 1959 — chronologically implausible for a serving Deputy Director, whereas this biography's subject (b. 1921, P.W.D. engineer in Malaya/Singapore since 1946) is age 38 with a career shape leading directly into the post. Position + place + exact years + initial agree independently.
would_overturn: Evidence of a second E. Adamson in the Singapore Public Works Department in the late 1950s (e.g., a staff-list entry showing two E. Adamsons concurrently, or a forename for the stint that contradicts this biography); or evidence that 'D.D.P.W.' in this biography denotes a post in the Federation of Malaya rather than Singapore, with a different person holding the Singapore Deputy Directorship in 1959-60.
notes: The deterministic colony/tenure/position FAILs are all downstream of one compiler error: the timeline inherited 'Malaya' for the 1955-1960 clauses, but the verbatim text reads 'P.W.D., S'pore, 1955', placing the senior career in Singapore. With that corrected, the biography's 'D.D.P.W., 1958-60' matches the stint's Deputy Director of Public Works, Singapore, 1959-1960 on position abbreviation, place, and years. Edition co-occurrence also holds in fact (bio appears in editions 1959 and 1960, the stint's editions) despite the mechanical FAIL. The two outside gate-compatible biographies (b. 1871, b. 1867) are ruled out on age.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: the 1940 record is a generic junior title ('2nd Class Clerks' in the Colonial Secretary's Department), which recurs constantly and could belong to any young Agostini, including the b.1914 C. Agostini (aged 26 in 1940, before his 1941 control-officer appointment), or some third relative; and the bio appears in only one edition (1951), giving no edition co-occurrence. This fails because the stint is anchored at its other end by a unique senior office: the 1952 staff list shows L. C. Agostini as 'Government Printer', and the biography records 'gov. printer, 1950' after 'asst. gov. printer, 1949' — there is one Government Printer per colony, and the title, colony, and date all agree. The initials 'L. C.' match Louis Colville (Cedar), whereas the b.1914 bio is a bare 'C' and his verbatim career (control officer 1941, aerodrome posts) never touches the printery or the Colonial Secretary's Department. The 1940 clerk record is consistent with the bio's 'clk., Trin., 1932' onward. A father/son pair sharing 'L. C.' and the Government Printer office in the same two-year window is not a realistic alternative.
would_overturn: A record of a different L. C. Agostini as Government Printer of Trinidad circa 1952, or evidence the 1940 2nd-class clerk and the 1952 Government Printer rows were wrongly fused into one stint (e.g. an intervening staff list showing two distinct L. C. Agostinis).
notes: Both stints were flagged CONTESTED, but the verbatim entries fully separate the two careers: one is civil aviation (unique 'Director of Civil Aviation and Aerodrome Superintendent' post matching the b.1914 bio's 1947 appointment), the other is the printery (unique 'Government Printer' post matching the b.1912 bio's 1950 appointment). All deterministic colony/position FAILs stem from the bios' events not resolving to 'Trinidad and Tobago' mechanically; the printed text places both careers in Trinidad. The corpus has 4 Agostini biographies and 7 staff-list officials, so the remaining namesakes presumably belong to other cases.

arguments_against: Strongest counter-case: this is a single-initial biography, so the stint could belong to a same-name relative (a son or brother, also 'M. Covington') serving in Ceylon's Medical Department in 1877-1883, or to a successor holding the same generic-sounding post. That hypothesis fails on several grounds. First, the corpus contains exactly one official and one biography with this surname, so no second Covington is attested anywhere in the staff lists or biographical sections. Second, the match is not on a generic junior title but on a specific named office: the bio's career culminates in 'assistant colonial surgeon' (1859, 1st class 1867) in Ceylon, and the stint is 'Assistant Colonial Surgeons' in Ceylon's Medical Department -- the exact office, exact colony, exact initial. Third, the 1883 edition prints both the biography and the staff listing simultaneously, i.e. the compilers of that very volume treated the biographical subject as the then-current Assistant Colonial Surgeon; a Record of Services entry with no death/retirement note alongside a current staff listing of the same name and post is the editors' own identification. Fourth, the career shape is continuous: a man promoted to assistant colonial surgeon 1st class in 1867 still being listed as Assistant Colonial Surgeon in 1877-1883 is the natural continuation, and his age (entered service 1849, so roughly 50s-60s by 1883) is unremarkable for a serving colonial surgeon. The deterministic FAILs are mechanical artifacts: tenure_overlap and position_sim fail only because the bio's placed events stop at 1867, before the stint window opens, and edition_cooccurrence fails despite both records demonstrably appearing in the 1883 edition.

evidence_cited.fact: Medical sub-assistant to the government of Ceylon

would_overturn: Evidence of a second M. Covington in Ceylon's medical service (e.g. a staff list or biography distinguishing 'M. Covington, sen.' and 'jun.', or two M. Covingtons in any Ceylon Medical Department listing), or a record showing the biographical subject died or left the service before 1877.
notes: All five deterministic similarity checks failed mechanically, but each failure is explained by the bio's compiled timeline ending in 1867 (no placed events overlap the 1877-1883 stint window) rather than by any contradiction. The verbatim texts agree on colony (Ceylon), department/profession (medical), specific office (assistant colonial surgeon), initial (M.), and 1883 edition co-occurrence, and the surname is unique in the corpus. The edition_cooccurrence FAIL appears to be a tooling artifact worth investigating: bio editions [1883] and stint editions [1877, 1878, 1879, 1880, 1883] plainly share 1883.

arguments_against: The bio carries a single initial ('C'), so the namesake trap applies: with 7 officials of this surname in the staff lists, another C. Agostini (or a relative whose forename starts with C, including Louis Colville Cedar Agostini, whose initials L.C.C. contain a C) could be the staff-list entry. The strongest counter-case is that Louis Colville Cedar Agostini claims this stint. That counter-case fails on position: the stint is 'Director of Civil Aviation and Aerodrome Superintendent' in every listed year 1949-1962, an essentially unique senior post, and Louis Colville Cedar's printed career is entirely in the government printery (clerk 1932, supervisor 1946, asst. gov. printer 1949, gov. printer 1950) with no aviation service at all; he cannot simultaneously be government printer and director of civil aviation in 1952. Meanwhile bio agostini_c_b1914 records appointment as 'dir. of civil aviation, and aerodrome supt., 1947' in Trinidad, exactly the stint's title, two years before the stint's first listing, with continuous listing through 1962 matching the bio's presence in editions 1948-1966. A hypothetical third C. Agostini holding this exact unique post in the same colony in the same years while the biography's holder vanishes from the staff lists is not a realistic alternative. The deterministic FAILs (colony, tenure, position_sim) are artifacts of the bio's events not being machine-placed to 'Trinidad and Tobago'; the verbatim text places the career in Trinidad ('T'dad. Govt. service.').

evidence_cited.fact: Director of Civil Aviation and Aerodrome Superintendent
dir. of civil aviation, and aerodrome supt., 1947

would_overturn: Evidence of a second C. Agostini in Trinidad civil aviation in 1949-1962 (e.g. a staff-list record distinguishing two C. Agostinis, or a forename for the stint-holder inconsistent with the b.1914 biography), or a death/retirement record for the b.1914 man before 1962 while the stint continues.
notes: Both stints were flagged CONTESTED, but the verbatim entries fully separate the two careers: one is civil aviation (unique 'Director of Civil Aviation and Aerodrome Superintendent' post matching the b.1914 bio's 1947 appointment), the other is the printery (unique 'Government Printer' post matching the b.1912 bio's 1950 appointment). All deterministic colony/position FAILs stem from the bios' events not resolving to 'Trinidad and Tobago' mechanically; the printed text places both careers in Trinidad. The corpus has 4 Agostini biographies and 7 staff-list officials, so the remaining namesakes presumably belong to other cases.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: Addison is a common surname in the Gold Coast (the corpus has 21 officials and 10 biographies with this surname), so the stint could belong to a different A. J. Addison — e.g. a relative who was also a Gold Coast engineer in 1949-51, while the biography's subject held a post not captured by this staff list. There is also no shared honour and no edition co-occurrence to corroborate. This counter-hypothesis fails to be compelling because the agreement is not name-only: both initials match exactly, the profession matches specifically (bio 'engrnr., 1948' vs staff-list position 'Engineers' — engineer, not a generic junior title like clerk or cadet), the colony matches, and the 1949-1951 listing sits exactly inside the bio's engineer phase (promoted engineer 1948, regional engineer 1955), with age 27 at stint start being entirely plausible. The edition_cooccurrence FAIL is expected, not contrary: the biography appears only in the 1956 edition while the stint is from the 1949/1951 staff lists, so no agreeing edition-years are possible. Still, because the only corroborating dimensions beyond initials are profession and timeline, and the surname is locally common, a second A. J. Addison engineer cannot be ruled out to publication standard, so this stays 'probable' rather than 'certain'.

evidence_cited.fact: A. J. Addison | Engineers | Posts and Telegraphs
A. J. Addison | Engineers | Engineering Branch

would_overturn: Evidence of a second A. J. Addison serving as an engineer in the Gold Coast circa 1949-1951 (e.g. another biography or staff-list entry with the same initials and profession); or a 1949/1951 staff-list detail incompatible with the biography, such as the Posts and Telegraphs engineer holding an honour, seniority date, or forename that contradicts a man born in 1922 who joined as probationary assistant engineer in 1943.
notes: Single-bio, single-stint case. All deterministic gates pass except honour (neither side lists any honour) and edition_cooccurrence, which is structurally impossible here: the bio is attested only in the 1956 edition, the stint only in 1949/1951. The stint years 1949-1951 fall precisely within the bio's 'engrnr.' phase (1948-1955), consistent with a 1943 probationary-assistant-engineer entrant born 1922. Held at 'probable' (human review) rather than 'certain' solely because the surname is locally common (21 staff-list officials) and corroboration rests on initials + profession + timeline without honours or co-occurrence.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: 'clerk' is a generic junior title, and the Windward Islands staff list covers several islands (Grenada, St. Vincent, Tobago, St. Lucia), so the 1877-78 'Clerk to Treasurer' could be a same-name relative serving in a different island's treasury; the deterministic colony check FAILED because no bio event resolves to 'Windward Islands', and there is no edition co-occurrence (bio printed only in 1883, stint listed 1877-1878). Why it fails: St. Lucia was administratively part of the Windward Islands group in this period, so the place evidence is consistent rather than contradictory; the position is not merely 'clerk' but specifically a treasurer's-office clerkship, exactly matching the bio's entire career arc (supernumerary clerk in the treasurer's office 1869, returned to the treasury as second clerk 1874, first clerk 1875); the initials J. L. agree with JOHN L. F.; and the 1877 start follows seamlessly from the bio's 1875 promotion. With only 7 officials of this rare surname in the corpus, a second J. L. Clavier holding the same specific treasury clerkship in the same island group two years after the bio's last event is an unlikely coincidence. Still, because the corroboration rests on one specific dimension (position) plus partial initials, and the staff list does not name St. Lucia explicitly, a same-name relative cannot be fully excluded, so this stays at probable rather than certain.

evidence_cited.fact: Supernumerary clerk in the treasurer's office, St. Lucia, March, 1869
returned to the treasury as second clerk, in June, 1874; first clerk, March, 1875

would_overturn: Evidence that the 1877-78 Windward Islands 'Clerk to Treasurer' post was located in Grenada, St. Vincent, or Tobago rather than St. Lucia; or discovery of a second contemporaneous J. L. Clavier (e.g. a brother) in Windward Islands service; or a record showing John L. F. Clavier left the treasury or the colony before 1877.
notes: Rare surname (7 officials, 2 bios in corpus) strengthens the Windward Islands assignment but cannot make it certain on its own: the staff list does not name St. Lucia explicitly and there is no edition co-occurrence. The British Guiana medical officer L. Clavier is very likely a distinct person, possibly a relative, and should block any future automated merge attempts based on surname alone.

arguments_against: Strongest case against the British Guiana stint: the bio appears only in the 1935 edition, so there is a 14-year gap with no intervening listings, and the 1949 Deputy Director could be a different J. P. Bruen (e.g. a same-name relative or a later recruit), with the deterministic tenure_overlap, position_sim and edition_cooccurrence checks all failing. This fails because the gap is mechanical (no editions of the bio between 1935 and 1949 to overlap), while the substance lines up on multiple independent dimensions: the bio's man was 'asst. engrn. to sea defence bd. of comsnrs.' in British Guiana in 1934 and the 1949 stint is 'Deputy Director Drainage, Irrigation and Sea Defences' in the same colony's Public Works Department — an extremely narrow specialty (sea defences in British Guiana), a natural 15-year promotion arc, initials J. P. matching the full forenames John Patrick, and age 45 in 1949, which fits a Deputy Director but rules out a son of a man born 1904 (such a son would be in his early 20s). The corpus holds only 3 officials with this surname. Strongest case against the Fiji stint: the bio never mentions Fiji, every place-based check fails, and the link rests on chaining through the British Guiana stint — a second J. P. Bruen engineer elsewhere in the service is conceivable. It is weakened because the Fiji 'Director of Public Works' appointment begins in 1950 exactly as the identically named British Guiana Public Works Deputy Director's listing ends in 1950, a textbook colonial-service promotion-with-transfer in the same engineering field; the 1950 double listing is the usual edition lag for a mid-year transfer. That counter-hypothesis is unlikely but realistic enough that the overall verdict is probable, not certain; on the British Guiana stint alone I would be at certain.

evidence_cited.fact: asst. engrn. to sea defence bd. of comsnrs., 1934
Deputy Director Drainage, Irrigation and Sea Defences

would_overturn: Evidence of two contemporaneous J. P. Bruens in the Colonial Office Lists (e.g. the British Guiana Deputy Director continuing in post after 1950 while the Fiji Director serves, or both appearing with distinct forenames/honours); a birth or service record showing the Fiji Director of Public Works was born in a different year than 1904; or confirmation that the external biography bruen_john-patrick_b1904 is a distinct individual rather than an OCR variant of this one.
notes: The bio surname 'BBUEN' is almost certainly an OCR error for 'BRUEN' (the verbatim entry reads 'BBUEN, JOHN PATRICK ... asst. engrn., Br. Guiana'). Both stints are flagged as gate-compatible with an out-of-case biography 'bruen_john-patrick_b1904' — same forenames, same birth year — which is very likely the same person under the corrected spelling. If that external bio already holds these stints, this case's bio should be merged/deduplicated with it rather than creating a parallel assignment; flagging for the pipeline since duplicate_bio_groups only covers in-case bios. The 1950 co-listing (British Guiana stint ends 1950, Fiji stint begins 1950) is consistent with a single mid-year transfer, not a contradiction.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: the dossier notes this stint is also gate-compatible with an out-of-case biography 'haggart_john-g_e1872', already linked elsewhere or filtered, and Phase 1 dropped this pair as ambiguous because of that comparable-strength competitor. If that other biography is a distinct John G. Haggart (e.g. a father/son pair), assigning here would be a false merge; and even if it is the same man, double-linking the stint to two biography entries violates the partition. Why the identity counter-hypothesis fails: the biography names two specific, unique-at-any-time Canadian cabinet offices — Postmaster-general (1888) and minister of railways and public works (1892) — and the staff-list stint holds exactly those offices in agreeing years (Postmaster-General in the 1889/1890 editions, Minister of Railways and Canals in 1894/1896), with matching J. G. initials in the same colony. Two same-named people cannot both have held these singular cabinet posts in those years, so 'haggart_john-g_e1872' is almost certainly a duplicate/variant entry for the same person rather than a different individual. The residual risk is bookkeeping (duplicate biography), not identity, which is why this is graded probable rather than certain and routed to human review.
would_overturn: Evidence that 'haggart_john-g_e1872' describes a different individual (e.g. a distinct birth year or a career incompatible with Postmaster-General 1888 / Railways 1892), or a record showing a second John G. Haggart holding either cabinet office in those years; alternatively, confirmation that the stint is canonically linked to the e1872 biography and the system forbids re-assignment.
notes: The biography is an OCR-truncated entry ('AGGART, THE HON. JOHN G.' = Haggart). Its two printed offices match the stint's unique cabinet posts exactly (position_sim 100 on 'Postmaster-general' ~ 'Postmaster-General'; bio's 'minister of railways and public works, 1892' corresponds to the staff list's 'Minister of Railways and Canals' from 1894 editions, edition lag expected). The flagged out-of-case biography haggart_john-g_e1872 is very likely a duplicate entry for the same person (cabinet offices are singular); reviewer should dedupe across cases rather than treat them as rivals. Edition_cooccurrence FAIL is an artifact: the bio appears only in 1897 and the stint's 1897 rows exist, but the mechanical check found no agreeing edition-years on the matched events.

arguments_against: Strongest case against the 1879 stint: the staff list prints only 'H. Boast' (single initial), 'Clerk' is the most generic junior title in the corpus, and 11 Boasts appear in the staff lists, so this could be a brother or cousin with the same first initial serving as a clerk elsewhere in Natal. This fails because the agreement is not title-alone: the bio places Henry W. precisely as a clerk attached to resident magistrates' courts in Natal continuously across 1878-1880 (Klip River from 26 May 1878, Weenen from Dec. 1880), and the stint is a Clerk in the Natal 'Magisterial Department and Staff' in exactly 1879-1880 - colony, department, role, and both bracketing years all agree; the only gate-compatible alternative bios (boast_henry-w_e1876-2, roast_henry-w_e1876) have ids indicating they are OCR/duplicate variants of this same Henry W. entry, not distinct people. Strongest case against the 1896 stint: a 13-16 year evidence gap (the bio appears only in the 1883 edition and ends Dec. 1880), so the 1896-1910 Magistrate could be a different H. W. Boast, e.g. a son. This is weakened because the initials match in full ('H. W. Boast' printed every year), the colony is the same, and the role is the natural promotion endpoint of the bio's career (clerk to resident magistrate -> Magistrate); a son of a man whose own career began only in 1876 would be roughly 20 years old in 1896, too young to hold a Natal magistracy. The counter-hypothesis is not dead, hence probable rather than certain, and the outside-case duplicate bios mean this assignment needs reconciliation in review.

evidence_cited.fact: clerk to resident magistrate, Klip River Division, 26th May, 1878
clerk and Zulu interpreter, Court of Resident Magistrate, county of Weenen, Dec. 1880
H. W. Boast | Magistrate | Native High Court

would_overturn: A printed birth year or a later Record of Services entry showing Henry W. Boast left the Natal service before 1896 or died before 1910; evidence of a second adult H. W. Boast (or H. Boast) serving concurrently in the Natal magisterial establishment in 1879-1880 or 1896-1910; or confirmation that boast_henry-w_e1876-2 / roast_henry-w_e1876 are distinct individuals rather than OCR duplicates of this biography.
notes: The two unassigned stints (South Africa 1912-1918 and Swaziland 1917-1927) overlap in 1917-1918 with identical Clerk roles in the Swaziland Administration Establishment and almost certainly belong to one person - likely a second, younger H. W. Boast (possibly a son of the bio subject), since Swaziland appears under the South Africa rubric in some editions. The dossier flags out-of-case gate-compatible bios boast_henry-w_e1876-2 and roast_henry-w_e1876 ('Roast' being a plausible OCR error for 'Boast'); their ids suggest OCR/duplicate variants of this same biography. If either is already linked to the Natal stints assigned here, deduplicate the biographies rather than treating them as competing persons.

arguments_against: The strongest case against: the biography's last printed event is 1866, and the stint begins 1879 — a 13-year gap. A son or other relative named J. Craw could have succeeded James in the same Natal office, and colonial offices did pass between kin; the staff list prints only the initial 'J', so it cannot distinguish James from, say, John. The place evidence is also weak in isolation: the 1866 event inherits 'Natal' from the previous clause rather than stating it. Why this fails: there is no gap in the post itself to suggest succession — the bio puts James as 'first clerk in the registry of deeds and stamp office' from 1866, and the staff list shows a J. Craw holding exactly that rank ('1st Clerk') in exactly that department ('Registrar of Deeds') in the same colony continuously through 1879-1883, the natural continuation of a single tenure. The corpus contains exactly one Craw official and one Craw biography, so no second candidate exists; and the 1883 edition prints the biography and the staff-list entry concurrently, which fits one living serving officer. The succession hypothesis requires inventing an undocumented second Craw who happens to hold the identical specific post; nothing in the dossier supports him. Still, because the link rests on a single-initial staff name plus position continuity across a 13-year listing gap, a realistic (if unevidenced) relative-successor hypothesis survives, so this stops short of certain.

evidence_cited.fact: first clerk in the registry of deeds and stamp office of that colony, 1866
1 official(s) with this surname in the graph's staff lists

would_overturn: Evidence of a second Craw in Natal (e.g. a staff list or biography showing two contemporaneous Craws, a death/retirement notice for James Craw before 1879, or a forename for the 1879-1883 clerk other than James) would reverse this assignment.
notes: The deterministic edition_cooccurrence check reports FAIL, but the dossier itself shows the biography in editions [1883, 1886] and the stint in editions [1879, 1880, 1883] — 1883 is shared, so the mechanical check appears wrong on this point; the 1883 co-occurrence mildly supports the link. Position match is highly specific: 'first clerk in the registry of deeds' (bio, 1866) vs '1st Clerk' in 'Registrar of Deeds' (staff list), same colony, and the surname is unique in the corpus. Held at probable rather than certain solely because the staff list carries a bare initial and there is a 13-year gap between the bio's last event and the stint's first listing.

arguments_against: Strongest case against: (1) The stint is also gate-compatible with biography 'sabine_neville-warde_b1910' outside this case, so assigning it here could double-assign it. But that bio shares the identical forenames 'Neville Warde' and identical birth year 1910 with this one, and 'ABINE' is plainly an OCR truncation of 'SABINE' (the stint surname is 'Sabine'); this is a duplicate bio record of the same individual, not a rival identity, so the assignment is not a false merge across distinct people. (2) There are 7 Sabines in the staff lists and a 15-year gap (1934 Gold Coast to 1949 North Borneo) with zero overlapping editions, so the North Borneo officer could be a different N. W. Sabine. However, the match is not surname-plus-one-initial: both initials N.W. agree with 'Neville Warde', the age fits (39 at stint start), and crucially the career is in the same specialised cadre - the bio shows entry to the colonial audit dept ('col. dit dept' is OCR for 'col. audit dept') and 'ast. audr., Gold Coast, 1934', while the stint is 'Principal Auditor' in the AUDIT department, a natural 15-year promotion within the unified Colonial Audit Service whose officers routinely transferred between colonies. A second coincidental N. W. Sabine in the colonial audit branch is implausible. (3) 'Auditor' alone could be a recurring title, but 'Principal Auditor' is a senior, department-specific post, not a generic junior title. The counter-hypotheses are real enough (evidence gap, external duplicate bio) to withhold 'certain', but the identification is clearly more likely than not.
would_overturn: A staff list or biography expanding the North Borneo officer's initials to forenames other than Neville Warde; evidence that Neville Warde Sabine died, retired, or left the Colonial Audit Service before 1949; or a colony-agreeing record placing him in a different territory during 1949-1951.
notes: Biography 'abine_neville-warde_b1910' is an OCR-truncated duplicate of 'sabine_neville-warde_b1910' (leading 'S' lost; same forenames, same birth year 1910); the dossier notes the stint is gate-compatible with that external bio and 'already linked elsewhere or filtered'. The two bio records should be merged upstream. If the stint is already linked to the sabine_ record, this verdict corroborates that link rather than contesting it; the reviewer should resolve the bio-level deduplication, not the identity. The deterministic FAILs (colony, tenure_overlap, position_sim, edition_cooccurrence) all stem from the bio appearing only in the 1937 edition, 12 years before the stint; the audit-branch continuity is visible only in the verbatim text ('col. dit dept' = colonial audit dept).

arguments_against: Strongest case against: (1) A same-name relative (brother or cousin) named N. M. Bharucha could have entered the Ceylon service around 1920, and the bio's only attested edition is 1915 with zero edition co-occurrence with the stint (1920-1937), plus a five-year listing gap. This fails because the corpus context shows only 1 official with this surname in the entire graph's staff lists, the stint's two initials match both forenames Navroji Maneckji exactly, the age fits perfectly (cadet at 24 in 1914, magistrate at 30 in 1920), and the career shape is the standard Ceylon civil service track: cadets appointed ~1914 routinely advanced to Police Magistrate / Commissioner of Requests / District Judge posts by 1920. The position_sim FAIL is an artifact of comparing the cadet-entry string to a later judicial title, not evidence of different people. (2) The stronger residual concern: the stint is also gate-compatible with the out-of-case biography 'bharucha_navroji-manekji_b1890', which is the same printed name one OCR character apart (Manekji vs Maneckji) with the identical birth year 1890 - almost certainly a duplicate record of the SAME human, not a rival person. So the live risk is not a wrong-person merge but a duplicate-person double-assignment if that variant bio was or will be linked to this stint elsewhere. That bookkeeping risk is why this is 'probable' (routed to human review) rather than 'certain'.

evidence_cited.fact: Commissioner of Requests and Police Magistrate

would_overturn: Evidence that 'bharucha_navroji-manekji_b1890' is a distinct person (different birth year, different career text, or both bios co-occurring as separate entries in the same edition) rather than an OCR variant; or discovery of a second N. M. Bharucha in Ceylon staff lists 1915-1920; or a printed entry showing Navroji Maneckji Bharucha left the Ceylon service before 1920.
notes: The dossier flags the stint as gate-compatible with out-of-case bio 'bharucha_navroji-manekji_b1890' (Manekji vs Maneckji, same birth year 1890). These two biographies are almost certainly OCR/spelling-variant duplicates of one person; they cannot be declared in duplicate_bio_groups because only one is in this case. Reviewer should verify the variant bio is not separately linked to this same stint, and merge the two bio records if confirmed duplicates. Identity of the stint with this human is very strong: unique surname in staff lists, exact two-initial match, Ceylon colony match, age 30 at stint start, and canonical cadet-to-judiciary Ceylon career progression.
